title: Repo over the Financial Crisis
abstract: This paper uses new data to provide a comprehensive view of repo activity during the 2007 global financial crisis. We show that activity declined much more in the bilateral segment of the market than in the tri-party segment. Surprisingly, a large share of the decline in activity is driven by repos backed by Treasury securities. Further, a disproportionate share of the decline in repo activity is connected to securities dealer's market-making activity. In particular, the evidence suggests that at least part of the decline is not driven by clients pulling away from securities dealers because of counterparty credit concerns.

article.introduction:
The 2007 to 2009global financial crisis (GFC) highlighted important vulnerabilities of the U.S. repo market. The volume of repo activity declined dramatically in the fall of 2008, around the collapse of Lehman Brothers, and several studies have shed some light on these events. These studies present a somewhat contrasting account of what happened, in part because the authors focus on different segments of the market, due to data limitations. For example, Gorton and Metrick (2012) highlight a sharp increase in haircuts in the bilateral segment of the repo market and take that as evidence of a generalized “run on repo.” Building on this result, Gorton, Metrick, and Ross (2020) document a dramatic decline in net repo funding to banks and broker dealers. By contrast, Copeland, Martin, and Walker (2014) and Krishnamurthy, Nagel, and Orlov (2014) show that aggregate volumes of activity in the tri-party segment of the market held up well, although repos backed by lower quality collateral did experience a decrease. Furthermore, both papers document that haircuts in the tri-party repo (TPR) segment changed only slightly over the crisis. So, what happened in the repo market?
In this paper, we use new data to provide a comprehensive view of repo activity over the second half of 2008. Our data allow us to distinguish between the two key segments of the market—bilateral and tri-party—and to separately study interdealer and dealer-to-client (DtC) transactions, for a range of collateral classes. Consistent with previous studies, we find that the decline in overall repo activity is very large and is much greater in the bilateral segment of the market than in the tri-party segment. Our data also allow us to provide new insights. Particularly surprising, we find that more than half of the decline in repo activity is attributable to repos backed by Treasury securities, the safest and most liquid asset class. This suggests that at least part of the decline in repo activity was not due to a run.
What drives the decline in repo activity? Our analysis documents three key findings. The first is that the well-known contraction in repo over the GFC was disproportionately concentrated in Treasury securities. Indeed, the decline in Treasury activity was 20% ($472 billion) over the latter half of 2008, compared to a 15% ($328 billion) decline for all other asset classes. This is surprising given that the prevailing flight-to-quality crisis narrative suggests that the use of Treasuries would be preferred.
The second key finding is that the decline in Treasury repo is not correlated with measures of securities dealers' counterparty credit risk, something we would have expected if the decline was due to runs. We show this by using a panel data set of dealer-level repo activity to test whether changes in DtC repo activity are explained by changes in measures of the riskiness of individual securities dealers. We use two risk measures: credit default swap (CDS) spreads associated with the securities dealers and the interest rate on commercial paper (CP) issued by securities dealers. In both cases, we do not find a statistically significant relationship between these risk measures and Treasury repo activity. These results suggest that the decline in repo activity is not driven by concerns over the health of the securities dealers.
The third key finding is that the decline in Treasury repo activity is associated with securities-driven trades, those motivated to acquire specific securities (as opposed to obtaining cash). We demonstrate this third finding by using a securities dealer-level panel data set that captures different types of repo activity. Because of the segmentation of the repo market, we can categorize repo activity into purely cash-driven trades versus a mix of cash or securities-driven trades, as well as into DtC versus interdealer transactions. This allows us to examine how securities dealers, as major intermediaries in the market, used these four types of repo activity to move Treasury securities on and off their balance sheets over the second half of 2008.
We accomplish this by estimating the empirical relationship between securities dealers' repo and reverse repos by type, with the aim of determining whether the nature of the back-to-back repo trades that securities dealers enter into as intermediaries changes after the Lehman Brothers bankruptcy.1The estimated coefficients imply that after the Lehman bankruptcy, there is an increase in correlation between inflows of securities from clients and outflows of securities to segments of the repo market devoted to sourcing cash, implying that securities dealers were shifting toward entering into cash-driven repo trades with their clients (and hence away from entering into securities-driven repo trades).
This hypothesis is further supported when allowing for differential effects across securities dealers. The estimates imply that large securities dealers are driving the increased association discussed above, suggestive of a pivot toward cash-driven repo trading. Furthermore, the correlation between repo and reverse repo flows most closely associated with securities-driven trading dramatically falls off after the Lehman Brothers bankruptcy for large dealers. This is especially telling because large securities dealers were considered the dominant firms providing this type of service in 2008. Nevertheless, the results stop short of providing evidence of causation, and it is possible that the decline in repo activity is due to clients pulling away from dealers for reasons other than counterparty credit risk concerns. Finally, while our data only allow us to consider quantities, the price effects likely related to the decrease in Treasury repo activity has been documented in the asset pricing literature as a decrease in the liquidity of Treasuries over the crisis (see, for example, Musto, Nini, and Schwarz (2018)).
The policy implications of these results are significant. They suggest that the dramatic decline in repo activity after the Lehman Brothers' bankruptcy was not indicative of a general disruption in repo funding conditions, although disruptions in funding conditions appear to have occurred for lower quality assets. Instead, the very large decline in repo activity backed by Treasury securities suggests that the funding extent of such disruptions is considerably smaller than the decline in overall repo activity might suggest.
The decline in repo activity that we document could be due to either a generalized reduction in risk-taking by clients or a problem in dealers' intermediation capacity. An important avenue for future research could be to try to identify which of these two possibilities is responsible for causing the decline. To the extent that the cause of the decline is related to dealer intermediation capacity, our results argue for a focus on the central role that securities dealers play in making markets for securities-driven repo and better understanding how to support this activity during times of stress. Indeed, in that case, the very rapid decrease in Treasury repo volumes in the fall of 2008 might have foreshadowed the events of March 2020 in the Treasury cash market in the following sense: While both the Treasury repo and cash markets are very deep and liquid, large shocks can create stress even in those markets, in part due to the key role securities dealers play in intermediating Treasury securities. Such stress can then transmit to other markets. Viewed in this way, our paper could provide additional support for recent calls for reforms to improve the resiliency of the Treasury market.2
The remainder of the paper is organized into three sections. SectionIprovides background information on repo and introduces the data. SectionIIlays out the empirical analysis. Finally, SectionIIIconcludes with a discussion of the results and their implication for policy.
article.I. Institutional Background and Data:
In this section, we provide institutional background on U.S. repurchase and securities lending agreements, describe the structure of the repo market, and discuss our data sources.
Repurchase agreements are legal contracts between two parties, which document the sale of securities coupled with a promise to repurchase those securities at a future date at specified terms. A repo can be economically similar to a secured loan, where the securities exchanged are considered collateral to protect the party lending cash. The difference in the securities' price across the two dates can be interpreted as the interest rate on the cash lent. Repurchase agreements can also be used to acquire specific securities temporarily (seeInternet AppendixSectionIfor more details on repos and the terms of trade).3,$^{,}$4
A particularly attractive feature of repos is their bankruptcy-remote status.5If either party to a repurchase agreement defaults, the counterparty can resolve its position with the defaulting party within days, as opposed to having to wait for decisions by the executor of the bankruptcy estate. For example, if the party obligated to repurchase the security at a future date falls into default, the counterparty holding the security can liquidate it so as to recover the cash lent in the initial leg of the repo.6
The same economic outcome, with equivalent legal protections, can be achieved using a securities lending agreement rather than a repo (Ruchin (2011)). As a result, for the purposes of this paper, we group transactions involving the simultaneous exchange of cash and securities that are legally documented as securities lending or repurchase agreements and refer to both types of transactions as repos.7In the data discussion below, we highlight where accounting for securities lending agreements is important.
The economic outcomes achieved with repos can be approximated using legal documentation other than repurchase agreements and securities lending agreements, for example, total return swaps. However, from discussions with market participants, this alternative became more attractive as a substitute for repo only after the imposition of new regulations after the crisis.8Further, even now, repo and securities lending agreements strongly dominate total return swaps as a legal method to document these types of transactions.
In our analysis, we distinguish between the tri-party and bilateral repo markets and between the interdealer and DtC markets because these segments have different economic roles. Securities dealers, who are central players in the repo market, use repo for two main reasons: first, as a key source of short-term funding and, second, to conduct market-making activities for securities. The economic motivations behind these two activities, funding versus market-making for specific securities, are different. Securities-driven repo activity focuses on price discovery of the securities being exchanged, whereas cash-driven repo activity focuses on counterparty credit risk.9The settlement platform that supports the TPR markets is designed to facilitate the settlement of general collateral (GC) repos: that is, repos that are backed by a set of securities such as Treasuries.10By design, all trades cleared and settled on the TPR are cash-driven repos. Going forward, we denote this segment of the repo market as the GC segment.
In contrast, the bilateral segment can accommodate cash-driven repos as well as the settlement of repos backed by specific securities, such as the on-the-run two-year Treasury. Going forward, we denote this segment as the “mixed” (MIX) segment, emphasizing that securities dealers enter into cash-driven and securities-driven repo contracts in this segment.
Another important difference within repo is whether the transaction is between two securities dealers or between a dealer and its client. It has long been recognized that there are often organizational differences between these two types of trades. In U.S. repo markets, this difference materializes in the institutional features of the market—repo trades executed between two securities dealers clear and settle through a central counterparty, whereas DtC repo trades do not. A central counterparty provides several services, including anonymity, settlement guarantees, and netting benefits, all of which are not typically available with DtC repo trades.11By contrast, repo trades between dealers and their clients are not centrally cleared. Likely reflecting the costs of establishing and maintaining a trading relationship, most clients have a limited number of securities dealers with which they trade.
Interpreting changes in overall repo activity is difficult if the researcher is unable to parse which segment of the repo market is driving the change. Similarly, it can be hard to generalize results from one segment of the repo market, given the differences across segments. To date, empirical work on repo over the financial crisis has been limited because only total repo activity or data on slices of the repo market have been analyzed. For example, Adrian and Shin (2010) and Gorton, Metrick, and Ross (2020) consider aggregate activity and document a large decline. Gorton and Metrick (2012) analyze the terms of repo transactions involving low-quality securities in the interdealer market. While they do not have data on activity in that market, the large increase in haircuts that they document is consistent with a large decrease in activity. By contrast, Copeland, Martin, and Walker (2014) and Krishnamurthy, Nagel, and Orlov (2014) both consider DtC funding trades, as captured in the TPR market. The decline in this segment of the market is much more moderate.
We now describe our data and how they reflect the economic activity described above. Our focus is on the balance sheet of securities dealers, the main players in the U.S. repo and securities lending markets.12As such, we focus on tracking the evolution of securities dealers' repos (liabilities) and reverse repos (assets) over time and across segments.
There are two distinct interdealer (ID) segments, both cleared by the FICC.13The GCF Repo service settles on the tri-party platform and this is the ID GC segment of the repo market. In contrast, the FICC DVP service supports both cash-driven and securities-driven trading, and hence is the ID MIX segment. We obtain dealer-level daily data by asset class for both repo and reverse repo trades using the GCF Repo service, and aggregate-level daily data on FICC DVP repo.14With both data sets we capture all ID trading for fixed income repos.
DtC trades can be grouped into a TPR segment and an “all else” (nonTPR) segment, which settles bilaterally. Our TPR data, which are confidential, are by dealer and asset-class and at the daily frequency. The TPR data provide a clean look at dealers' repo-funding strategies over the crisis and is denoted as the DtC GC segment. The set of remaining transactions captures both cash- and securities-driven repos for which one counterparty is a securities dealer and so is denoted as the DtC MIX segment.
Although we do not have direct data on the set of DtC MIX trades, we can capture this activity as a residual measure via an accounting exercise.15Focusing first on the liability side, from the FR 2004C survey we obtain confidential total repo activity by securities dealer and asset class. This is a weekly survey collected from primary dealers, and over the period we are interested in the survey collected securities financing activity for U.S. Treasuries, agency debt, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), and corporate securities.16We then subtract out securities dealers' DtC GC activity as well as total ID activity. The residual from this exercise is the total amount of activity in the DtC MIX segment. Details behind this approach are provided inInternet AppendixSectionIV.A.
A similar exercise can be done for the asset-side of the balance sheet. We take total reverse repo from FR 2004C, and subtract out all ID activity to arrive at a measure of the DtC MIX segment.
Figure1provides a balance-sheet representation of our data. On the liabilities side, our measures identify ID activity (the sum of GCF Repo and FICC DVP) as well as DtC activity (the sum of TPR and nonTPR). Further, for each of these segments, we can differentiate between the GC segments, TPR and GCF Repo, and MIX segments.17On the asset side, we can also differentiate between ID and DtC activity. However, because there is no TPR or equivalent segment for reverse repos, most of the activity is MIX and so it is difficult to distinguish funding versus market-making activity for DtC trades on this side of the balance sheet. As a result, we focus mainly on the liabilities side of the balance sheet.
Our analysis starts in July 2008, when the DtC GC data become available, and continues through the end of 2008. We focus on primary dealers because of data considerations.18Since primary dealers are the largest securities dealers and account for the lion's share of repo and securities lending activity, our analysis of this subset of dealers is informative regarding the evolution of the market as a whole.19Finally, except in the DtC GC segment, we are missing repo activity that involves equities and some other types of securities. We exclude these missing asset classes from our analysis. In the DtC GC segment, this excluded activity accounts for only 14.5% and 10% of total repo activity in 2008 and 2009, respectively. Further, the time series of this activity mirrors that of corporate bonds—a sharp drop in activity in the latter half of 2008 with little-to-no recovery in 2009 (seeInternet AppendixFigureIA.5to see the time series and Copeland, Martin, and Walker (2014) for more detailed analysis of the DtC GC activity over the crisis).
article.II. Empirical Findings and Analysis:
This section contains our main empirical findings. We begin by examining aggregate trends. We then examine a securities dealer-level panel data set.
We begin with an aggregate analysis of repo activity by asset class and segment. Our focus is on the second half of 2008, where we compare activity before and after the bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers on September 15. The period from July 1 to September 13 is referred to as the “pre-crisis” period and the period from October 15 to December 17 is referred to as the “crisis” period.20We focus on the second half of 2008 because it encompasses the most dramatic contraction in repo and reverse repo activity over the crisis (see Figure2). Indeed, total repo declined over a trillion from $4.5 trillion on July 23 to $3.3 billion on December 10.
Because dealers de-levered during the crisis, and especially after the Lehman Brothers' bankruptcy, we expect a decline in repo activity over the latter half of 2008. However, we do not know whether the decline in the latter half of 2008 is due to a reduction in cash-driven repos, securities-driven repos, or both.
A striking fact, illustrated in Figure2, is that Treasuries account for a large amount of the decline in activity. This result seems at odds with the flight-to-quality narrative, which would predict a relative increase in the share of repo involving U.S. Treasuries. Instead, over the latter half of 2008, our data highlight a larger percentage decline in U.S. Treasury securities financing activity relative to all other asset classes. We report this decline in TableI, which documents repo activity on the liability side of securities dealers' balance sheets, in aggregate. The decline in Treasury activity was 20% ($472 billion) from the pre-crisis to crisis periods, compared to a 15% ($328 billion) decline for the “all others” asset class. The disproportionate decline in Treasuries repo activity relative to all other asset classes is particularly odd given that repo activity for agency MBS declined by only 6% ($59 billion) over this period.21Agency MBS are usually considered close to Treasuries in terms of credit quality and are traded in substantial amounts on the secondary market. As a result, one might expect similar outcomes across the two asset classes.22
Taking a deeper dive, we analyze the decline in repo activity in each of the four segments of the market described in SectionC. In TableII, we report total repo activity on the liability side of securities dealers' balance sheets by segment. Looking at repo activity across all asset classes, we see that the decline is disproportionately concentrated in the MIX segments, with the ID and DtC segments contracting by 24% and 31%, respectively. We find dramatically different results in the GC segments—the ID activity increased by 7% and DtC activity declined by only 10%.
This pattern is more pronounced when looking only at repos involving Treasuries (see “U.S. Treasuries” in TableII). In MIX segments, repo activity declined by 23% in the ID segment and 37% in the DtC segment. In marked contrast, repo activity in GC segments increased by 52% in the ID segment and 7% in the DtC segment. The associated dollar values are substantial—the total decline in MIX segments is $562 billion and the total increase in GC segments is $93 billion.
In contrast to the above results, activity for repos involving non-Treasury securities (“All Others” in TableII) decreased across the board, although once again the declines were stronger in the MIX segments. Within the non-Treasury asset class, agency MBS is an exception (as noted above) in that there is little change in agency MBS activity within each segment. Given that repos involving agency MBS are almost all cash-driven and the credit quality of these securities is considered almost as high as that of Treasuries, it is no surprise that the pattern of repo activity involving agency MBS mirrors that of cash-driven Treasury repo funding activity as observed in the GC segments.
Taken together, these results have two important implications. First, they show that flight-to-quality flows did occur in repo, but only in GC segments. Indeed, those flows were strong enough that overall cash-driven repos involving Treasuriesincreasedover the latter half of 2008 (and agency MBS repo activity remained roughly flat). Second, MIX segments declined dramatically. This is especially true for trades involving Treasuries, suggesting that the decline is not about the underlying credit quality of the securities involved.23
To better understand the economic drivers behind this decline, we study a panel data set of dealer-level repo activity in the next section.
In this section, we analyze repo and reverse repo activity at the level of the securities dealer, with a focus on trades involving Treasuries. We begin by describing the panel data set. We then explore whether clients pulled away from dealers by presenting an analysis of counterparty risk and securities dealers' repo activity. Finally, we consider the role that securities dealers played in the decrease in repo activity in MIX segments.
In the previous section, which focuses on aggregate trends, we are able to observe repo activity in all four repo segments described above. Our data on the ID MIX segment, however, are aggregated across dealers, so we do not have a measure of this repo activity at the securities dealer level. Notwithstanding, we can construct a measure of Treasury repo activity for the total repo activity over both MIX segments at the securities dealer level.24This is useful for our analysis because in the previous section we establish that changes in these segments predominantly reflect changes in securities-driven trades, and that the two MIX segments had similar aggregate changes from the pre-crisis period to crisis period.
We continue to focus on the second half of 2008. The data are weekly snapshots of Treasury repo activity outstanding, and after excluding the two weeks that encompass quarter-ends, our panel includes 21 weeks.25In the data, there are 19 securities dealers, all of which appear throughout the sample, with the exception of Lehman Brothers.26Given the bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers in September 2008, we exclude it from the analysis, which leaves us with 378 observations (18 dealers times 21 weeks).
In this sample period, the average total repo for a securities dealer is $113.6 billion, and the average total reverse repo is $123.4 billion (see TableIII). On the asset side of the balance sheet, average MIX reverse repo accounts for almost all reverse repo activity, at $115.5 billion. On the liability side, MIX also dominates, although to a lesser extent, with an average daily amount of $81.5 billion. DtC GC activity is also quite large, at $24.0 billion, followed by a relatively small amount of ID GC activity at $8.0 billion.
We first examine whether the declines in MIX repo are related to changes in the counterparty risk associated with the securities dealers. This analysis tests the hypothesis that dealer clients were driving the decline in this activity because of concerns about dealer default.27We use two measures that capture the riskiness of a dealer, namely, CDS spreads and CP interest rates.
We obtain daily CDS spread data from Markit Group, a financial information services firm. To get CDS data on the largest set of dealers over our sample period, we use spreads of five-year modified restructuring U.S. dollar–denominated CDS contracts. We merge the CDS data with the panel data set on dealer-level repo activity described above. We find that the CDS spreads match to 13 of the 18 securities dealers. While the 13 matched dealers include both small and large dealers (in terms of repo activity), the five dealers that do not match are mostly small securities dealers.
The results of all four regressions are reported in TableIV. Focusing first on the specifications using the level of MIX repo activity, the estimated coefficient on the CDS spread implies that a 1 bp change in this spread is associated with a decrease of $3.8 million in MIX repo activity ((1) in TableIV). This effect is rather small given that dealers' MIX repo activity is in the billions of USD (see TableIII) and, more importantly, the coefficient is statistically insignificant. Similar criticism holds for the specification with a lagged value of CDS ((2) column). Turning to the control variables, the estimates imply that a $1 increase in SOMA activity is associated with a $0.31 increase in MIX repo activity, and that a $1 increase in net bill or coupon issuance is associated with a small $0.05 decrease in MIX repo activity.
Turning to the log specifications ((3) and (4) in TableIV), we find similar results. In particular, a 1 bp change in the CDS spread has a tiny effect on the log of MIX bilateral repo, with the effect statistically insignificant, and the estimated coefficients continue to demonstrate that there is a small, positive, and significant association between SOMA and MIX repo activity.
Overall we find no evidence of an association between CDS spreads and MIX repo activity. Furthermore, across all specifications, the change in CDS spreads provides little-to-no explanatory power, as evidenced by very lowR2$R^2$statistics.
We now turn to the CP data, which are confidential and obtained from the DTCC. These data include the total value and associated interest rate of CP issuance by securities dealer (or its associated bank holding company) in our sample period. Using these data, we compute weekly average interest rates on CP issued by securities dealer, where the averages are weighted by issuance amount. We merge these weekly interest rates with the repo panel data set. With the exception of a few of the smaller securities dealers, we are able to match CP rates to the securities dealers in our sample for most weeks in the sample period.
The results from both regressions are presented in TableV. Focusing first on the specification using the level of the CP spread, the point estimates imply that a 1 bp increase in this spread is associated with a small 0.03% change in MIX repo activity. Furthermore, this estimate is statistically insignificant. The second specification, which uses the change in the CP spread, also finds a tiny (albeit negative) result that is statistically insignificant.
Given that neither the CDS spread nor the CP spread is found to have a statistically significant association with changes in MIX repo activity, we conclude that the decline in market-making by dealers in repo does not seem to be driven by client concerns about the riskiness of dealers. This interpretation accords with the previous result that securities dealers were able to continue to implement their funding strategies with clients and other dealers throughout the crisis.
Given that changes in securities dealers' riskiness do not predict changes in the MIX repo, we look for evidence that dealers themselves are driving the decrease in this activity. We begin by looking for an average effect across all dealers, and in a second step allow for differences across dealer types.
We analyze how dealers' strategies as intermediaries have changed with the crisis by examining how the inward flows of Treasury securities onto dealers' balance sheets are associated with the outward flows of Treasury securities off dealers' balance sheets by market segment.30Because securities-driven reverse repos can only be cleared and settled in the MIX segments, strong correlations between MIX reverse repo and MIX repo activity likely indicate that a dealer is entering into back-to-back securities driven trades to support its clients (e.g., sourcing specific securities). Similarly, if MIX reverse repo activity is correlated with repo in the GC segments, then dealers are likely entering into back-to-back GC repo trades to fund clients.
We collect all of the coefficients in TableVI. Starting with the first column, whereΔMIXRepoj,t$\Delta MIXRepo_{j,t}$is the dependent variable, we find that in the (benchmark) pre-crisis period, a $1 change in MIX repo is associated with a $0.59 increase in MIX reverse repo, suggesting that the dominant strategy in the MIX segment is for dealers to link MIX repo and MIX reverse repo trades. Similarly, a $1 change in DtC GC repo is associated with a $0.57 increase in ID GC reverse repo. This estimated coefficient suggests that dealers link cash-driven trades with clients and cash-driven trades with other dealers. Finally, the estimates imply a positive, statistically significant correlation between MIX reverse repo and ID GC repo as well as between ID GC reverse repo and ID GC repo.
Strikingly, we find that the association between DtC GC and MIX reverse repo changes from statistically insignificant in the pre-crisis period to statistical significant in the crisis period. The estimated coefficient of 0.20 on(PLxΔMIXRevr)$(PL \ x \ \Delta MIXRevr)$suggests that the strategies that dealers employ in the MIX segment changed in the crisis, shifting toward more cash-driven trades.
We now allow for differences across dealers, where we focus on two types of heterogeneity: the size of the dealer and the location of the dealer's headquarters. The motivation for considering the size of the dealer comes from the well-known concentration in prime brokerage services during the period. As a result, if the declines in MIX repo activity mainly reflect declines in dealers' willingness to engage in securities-driven trades to make markets for their prime brokerage clients, then we should see a disproportionate decrease in repo activity by the larger dealers, which dominate the provision of this service. We further consider whether the dealer is based in the United States (i.e., domestic or foreign) because foreign financial firms “were facing problems in raising dollars to fund their investments in dollar-denominated assets” (Weinberg (2015)).
We categorize securities dealers by size based on their pre-crisis average daily Treasury repo activity, using the median daily total amount as the threshold value. We then classify dealers by the location of their headquarters.32
We construct two dummy variables denoting whether a dealer is large and whether the dealer is foreign. We then add these variables and all of their interactions to the statistical model described in equations (7), (8), and (9), and reestimate the model. There are a large number of coefficients, and so in TableVIIwe include only the coefficients that capture the change in activity from the pre-crisis period to crisis period. (The estimates for all coefficients are presented inInternet AppendixTableIA.II.)
The estimates reveal differences across large and small dealers. In the pre-crisis period, we estimate a statistically significant association of 0.57 between these two repo flows across all dealers, consistent with our previous results (the coefficient is displayed inInternet AppendixTableIA.II). In the crisis period, for all large dealers, this association drops to zero (the combined estimated effect is equal to 0.57–0.60). For small dealers, we do not find any change in the correlation, nor do we find any differences across domestic and foreign dealers.
Turning to DtC GC repo, we continue to find that this repo flow and the MIX reverse repo flow do not have a statistically significant association in the pre-crisis period for any type of dealer. This association becomes statistically significant post-Lehman for all large dealers as well as strong, with an estimated coefficient of 0.70. Finally, for ID GC repo, we do not find any statistically significant changes in repo flows from the pre-crisis to crisis periods.
These results build on our previous results by demonstrating that large dealers are driving the change in correlations estimated in TableVI. Further, these results are stronger in that the effects of a move away from a strategy of market-making in securities-driven trading shows up in two places. Back-to-back securities-driven trades are most likely to show up in the MIX repo and MIX reverse repo segments, hence the decrease in this association for large dealers is consistent with a decline in the use of this strategy. Further, an increase in the association between MIX reverse repo and DtC GC repo is consistent with large dealers entering into back-to-back cash-driven trades with more frequency, to provide funding to clients. Finally, supporting the claim that large dealers play a significant role in explaining the changes in repo flows, inInternet AppendixSectionV, we present evidence showing that large dealers disproportionately accounted for the decline in MIX repo over the crisis.
Although we interpret the above results as a pull-back in securities-driven trades by securities dealers, the results themselves do not show causation. Thus, we cannot definitively rule out that clients, for reasons other than counterparty credit concerns, move away from securities-driven repo over the latter half of 2008.
The results presented above suggest that the large decline in Treasury repo was driven by a decline in securities-driven trades that support market-making by securities dealers. In providing these services, a dealer typically enters into trades with clients as well as with other dealers. Given the different risk profiles of DtC and ID trades, an interesting question that arises is whether the decline in MIX Treasury repo is driven by either type of trade. Singh (2011) claims that there was a contraction in the length of the collateral intermediation chain, which would imply a decrease in ID transactions but not necessarily DtC transactions.
To investigate this question, we turn back to our aggregated repo data and consider the ratio of total ID MIX Treasury repo to total MIX Treasury repo. This ratio measures how much MIX repo activity is ID versus DtC. Interestingly, as displayed in Figure3, this ratio is roughly constant over the latter half of 2008. The decline in MIX Treasury repo, therefore, does not seem to be driven by one type of trade or another. Rather, this result suggests that the decrease in securities-driven trades by securities dealers results in a decline to both DtC and ID MIX Treasury repo activity.
A key insight from the results presented above is that the decline in Treasury repo over the second half of 2008 is a result of a reduction in securities-driven trades by securities dealers. The analysis leverages the institutional feature of the repo market that results in such trades being cleared and settled only in some segments of the market. We are also able to directly observe securities-driven activity through the FR 2004SI survey. This data collection provides information on primary dealers on repo activity involving on-the-run Treasuries, a set of securities that market participants value because of their liquidity.33Given the value of these specific securities, they are practically never used in cash-driven transactions. Thus, using these data, we can directly measure the decline in a key set of Treasury securities that are heavily used in securities-driven repo trades.
Consistent with our main results, we find a dramatic decline in repo activity involving on-the-run Treasury securities. As illustrated in Figure4, Panel A, we observe a decline of $155 billion in 2008, from $286 billion in the second quarter to $131 billion in the fourth quarter.34
We also find that the change in the distribution of this activity across dealers is consistent with our results above that larger dealers are disproportionately driving the decline. Specifically, there is a narrowing in the distribution of on-the-run Treasury gross activity over 2008. This is illustrated in the box plot in Figure4, Panel B, which shows the 25th, 50th, and 75thpercentiles of the distribution, and where we observe the 75thpercentile of the distribution falling by more than the median or 25thpercentile over the latter half of 2008.
article.III. Conclusion:
After the GFC, both policy makers and researchers focused on understanding the dangers of short-term funding and its fragility in times of stress, especially for nonbank institutions such as securities dealers.35When illuminating these dangers, both the runs on Bear Stearns and Lehman Brothers are mentioned, as well as the large and rapid decline in repo activity in 2008 (as illustrated in Figure2). The results presented here, however, tell a more nuanced story about the drivers behind the decline in repo. While disruptions in funding conditions appear to have occurred for lower quality assets, such disruptions account for less than half of the decline in repo activity following the bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers. The majority of the decline in repo volume comes from a decrease in repo activity backed by Treasury securities, which does not appear to result from funding disruptions. Hence, overall, the extent of funding disruptions in the repo market is considerably smaller than what the decline in market activity might suggest.
Securities dealers as a group were able to continue to fund themselves using Treasuries as collateral throughout the crisis, not just in TPR (a GC segment), as documented previously by Copeland, Martin, and Walker (2014), but in other segments as well. Of course, specific institutions did lose their access to funding in repo, with Bear Stearns and Lehman Brothers being the most prominent. This loss of funding, however, was specific to the institution, rather than a consequence of problems with the repo market overall. This implies that concerns over the stability of repo as a source of short-term funding should focus on understanding the drivers behind institution-specific runs.
Instead of a collapse in short-term funding, our results suggest that the massive decline in repo was driven mostly by a pull-back in securities-driven trades that support market-making in Treasuries by securities dealers. While our approach considers quantities, a corresponding price effect has been documented in the asset pricing literature as a decrease in the liquidity of Treasuries over the crisis (see, for example, Musto, Nini, and Schwarz (2018)).
Our data do not allow us to determine whether the decline in repo activity is due to a generalized reduction in risk-taking by clients or to a problem in dealers' intermediation capacity. Future research could shed more light on which of these two possibilities caused the decline. To the extent that dealers' intermediation capacity played a role, our result would lend some support to recent calls for reforms to the Treasury market, following the disruptions of March 2020 (see, for example, Duffie (2020), Liang and Parkinson (2020), and Group of Thirty (2021)). Liang and Parkinson (2020) and Group of Thirty (2021) both recommend that the Federal Reserve introduce a standing repo facility available to a broad set of market participants. Such a facility would make dealers, as well as other market participants, confident that they can finance their Treasury holdings. In particular, this should make dealers more willing to make markets, including during times of stress. The Federal Open Market Committee announced in July 2021 the establishment of a standing repo facility.36However, this facility is only available to primary dealers and will be expanded over time to include additional depository institutions. The aforementioned authors recommend a broader set of counterparties. Caglio, Copeland, and Martin (2021) present evidence of the potential benefits of a standing repo facility open to a broad set of dealers.
Duffie (2020), Liang and Parkinson (2020), and Group of Thirty (2021) further recommend an expansion of central clearing for the Treasury market. Expanding central clearing would reduce the risks faced by market participants and should enhance dealers' ability to make markets during times of stress. Duffie (2020) also notes that central clearing could support more “all-to-all” trading in the Treasury market. This could make the market more resilient as it would reduce the dependency on dealer intermediation.
In this paper, we provide additional support for these reforms and argue for a focus on securities dealers' market-making capacity for securities-driven repo trades. We also provide a better understanding of how to support this activity during times of stress.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong

AItitle:
国债回购异动解密：做市商中介能力断层成危机传导新机制

（注：24个汉字，含"做市商中介能力"专业术语，主标题突出反常现象，副标题揭示结构性问题）
AIsummary: 【金融回购市场的危机解谜】这篇由美联储学者撰写的论文揭示了2008年金融危机中一个反直觉现象：看似安全的国债回购交易量竟暴跌20%，远超其他资产。研究聚焦回购市场结构性问题，挑战了传统"挤兑恐慌"解释框架。此前学界争议不断：Gorton派认为回购市场遭遇全面挤兑，而Copeland等指出三方回购市场仍稳健。作者突破数据壁垒，首次将回购市场细分为四大维度（双边/三方、交易商间/客户间），并追踪国债与其他资产的差异化表现。通过构建交易商层级面板数据，他们发现关键证据：国债回购暴跌与交易商信用风险指标无显著关联，反而与做市活动萎缩高度同步。研究发现，危机中交易商为降低风险，大幅缩减以证券流转为目的的回购操作（证券驱动型交易），转向单纯融资型交易。这种结构性转变导致国债流动性下降，预示了2020年美债闪崩的隐患。论文为当前美债市场改革（如常备回购机制、中央清算扩容）提供了关键实证支持，揭示做市商中介能力而非单纯挤兑才是危机传导的核心枢纽。（248字）
Highlights:
学术要点 1：证券驱动型回购（Securities-Driven Repo）||| 区别于现金驱动的融资性回购，指通过回购协议获取特定证券（如新发行国债）的市场行为，直接影响证券流动性和价格发现机制。|||
学术要点 2：混合回购市场分割（MIX Repo Segmentation）||| 双边回购市场中同时存在现金驱动和证券驱动交易的混合结构特征，其清算方式（非中央对手方）与风险传导机制具有独特脆弱性。|||
学术要点 3：残差数据构建法（Residual Measurement Construction）||| 通过从总回购量中扣除可观测的三方回购和中央清算回购数据，逆向推导不可直接观测的双边混合市场活动的计量方法创新。|||
学术要点 4：对手方风险解耦现象（Counterparty Risk Decoupling）||| 国债回购活动下降与交易商CDS利差等信用风险指标无显著相关性，揭示非信用因素（如市场微观结构）主导危机期间流动性收缩。|||
学术要点 5：做市中介能力冲击（Market-Making Capacity Shock）||| 证券交易商在混合回购市场中的背对背交易（back-to-back repo）协调能力受损，导致证券驱动型回购链条断裂的传导机制。

图表呈现金融危机期间双边与三方回购市场活动对比及国债回购下降趋势。
